subject: New Stanford Online Learning Opportunities!
body:
[1][jpeg]

[2]Stanford Online

NEW STANFORD ONLINE LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES!

[3]

[4]BUSINESS & MANAGEMENT

[5][jpeg]
[6]GIVING 2.0: THE MOOC
Starts October 6
This six-week course will teach you how to assess nonprofits, create a
high-impact philanthropic strategy, volunteer more effectively, use existing,
free technology for good, and more.
[7]Learn More >
[8][jpeg]
[9]ORGANIZATIONAL ANALYSIS
Self-Paced
How do we make sense of organizations and the challenges they face, let alone
develop means of managing them in desired directions? This is a self-paced
course that you can take at your leisure. Participants will learn multiple
theories of organizational behavior and apply them to actual cases of
organizational change.
[10]Learn More >
[11][jpeg]
[12]STOCKS AND BONDS: RISKS AND RETURNS
Self-Study, Starts October 13
Stocks and bonds have always been a critical part of any investment portfolio,
but what do investors actually get in exchange for their investment? Why do
publicly traded stocks and bonds have value? This course presents an overview of
stocks and bonds, with a focus on the finance fundamentals behind these
instruments.
[13]Learn More >
[14]EDUCATION

[15][jpeg]
[16]CONSTRUCTIVE CLASSROOM CONVERSATIONS: MASTERING LANGUAGE FOR COLLEGE AND
CAREER READINESS (ELEMENTARY)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course looks closely at student-to-student conversations and
addresses ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of
interactions described in the new standards. It consists of four sessions with
three weeks between each session. Learning in this course relies heavily on
participant contributions and comments, especially in the team collaboration
setting.
[17]Learn More >
[18][jpeg]
[19]CONSTRUCTIVE CLASSROOM CONVERSATIONS: MASTERING LANGUAGE FOR COLLEGE AND
CAREER READINESS (SECONDARY)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course is focused on the Common Core State Standards and Next
Generation Science Standards, which emphasize improving the quality of
student-to-student discourse as a major feature of instruction. The sessions and
assignments are designed for participants who teach or have access to classrooms
in which they can gather samples of students’ conversation during lessons, and
learn ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions
described in the new standards. The course includes resources and tasks for
instructional coaches and others who support teachers and build school-wide
capacity.
[20]Learn More >
[21][jpeg]
[22]SUPPORTING ENGLISH LANGUAGE LEARNERS UNDER THE NEW STANDARDS
Starts October 1
In this seven-week course, participants will use a range of practical tools for
gathering and analyzing language samples that show how students currently
construct claims supported by evidence and/or reasoning.
[23]Learn More >
[24]ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

[25][jpeg]
[26]ALGORITHMS: DESIGN AND ANALYSIS, PART I
Starts October 13
This six-week course covers fundamental principles of algorithm design. Learn
the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications to fast sorting,
searching, and multiplication. Learn several blazingly fast primitives for
computing on graphs, such as how to compute connectivity information and
shortest paths. Study how allowing the computer to "flip coins" can lead to
elegant and practical algorithms and data structures.
[27]Learn More >
[28][jpeg]
[29]AUDIO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR MUSIC APPLICATIONS
Starts October 1
Audio signal processing is an engineering field that focuses on the
computational methods for intentionally altering sounds, methods that are used
in many musical applications. This ten-week course focuses on spectral
processing techniques of relevance for the description and transformation of
sounds, developing the basic theoretical and practical knowledge with which to
analyze, synthesize, transform and describe audio signals in the context of
music applications.
[30]Learn More >
[31][jpeg]
[32]COMPUTER SCIENCE 101
Self-Paced
This self-paced course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course
materials at your own pace. Computer Science 101 teaches the essential ideas of
Computer Science for a zero-prior-experience audience. Topics include the nature
of computers and code, how computer hardware works, how software works,
structured data, computer security, analog vs. digital, digital media, images,
sounds, video, compression, and more.
[33]Learn More >
[34][jpeg]
[35]GAME THEORY
Starts October 5
Game theory is the mathematical modeling of strategic interaction among rational
(and irrational) agents. This nine-week course covers the basics: representing
games and strategies, the extensive form (which computer scientists call game
trees), Bayesian games (modeling things like auctions), repeated and stochastic
games, and more.
[36]Learn More >
[37][jpeg]
[38]INTRODUCTION TO CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
Self-Study
This self-study resource provides you with the opportunity to learn at your own
pace. This course is an overview of Chemical Engineering conducted through
discussion and engineering analysis of physical and chemical processes.Topics
include overall staged separations, material and energy balances, concepts of
rate process, energy and mass transport, and kinetics of chemical reactions.
[39]Learn More >
[40][jpeg]
[41]INTRODUCTION TO HAPTICS
Self-Paced, Starts October 1
This self-paced course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course
materials at your own pace. Paticipants will learn how to build, program, and
control haptic devices, which are mechatronic devices that allow users to feel
virtual or remote environments. Topics include an introduction to haptic
technology and human haptics, how to program virtual environments, mechanical
characterization and simulation, and how to assemble mechanical systems, make
circuits, and program Arduino-based micro-controllers.
[42]Learn More >
[43][jpeg]
[44]INTRODUCTION TO LOGIC
Starts September 29
Learn how to formalize information and reason systematically to produce logical
conclusions in this eight-week course. Instructors will also examine logic
technology and its applications — in mathematics, science, engineering,
business, and law.

[45]Learn More >
[46][jpeg]
[47]MACHINE LEARNING
Started September 22
Machine learning is the science of getting computers to act without being
explicitly programmed. In this 10-week course, participants will learn about the
most effective machine learning techniques, and gain practice implementing them.
Topics include supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and best practices in
machine learning.
[48]Learn More >
[49][jpeg]
[50]MINING MASSIVE DATASETS
Starts September 29
This seven-week course teaches algorithms for extracting models and other
information from very large amounts of data, with an emphasis on techniques that
are efficient and that scale well.
[51]Learn More >
[52][jpeg]
[53]QUANTUM MECHANICS FOR SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS
Starts September 30
This course aims to teach quantum mechanics to anyone with a reasonable
college-level understanding of physical science or engineering.
Topics include an introduction to quantum mechanics, Schroedinger's wave
equation, getting "quantum" behavior, quantum mechanics of systems that change
in time, measurement in quantum mechanics, writing down quantum mechanics
simply, the hydrogen atom, and how to solve real problems.
[54]Learn More >
[55]ENTREPRENEURSHIP

[56][jpeg]
[57]TECHNOLOGY ENTREPRENEURSHIP PART II
Started September 22
This four-week course is the second half of a course on the fundamentals of
technology entrepreneurship. Topics include how to create and verify a plan for
gathering resources such as talent and capital, create and verify a business
model for how to sell and market an entrepreneurial idea, and generalize this
process to an entrepreneurial mindset of turning problems into opportunities
that can be used in larger companies and other settings.
[58]Learn More >
[59]LAW

[60][jpeg]
[61]SURVEILLANCE LAW
Starts October 14
This six-week course explores how American law facilitates electronic
surveillance – but also substantially constrains it. Topics include the basics
of surveillance law, applying surveillance law to information technology,
compelled assistance to law enforcement, the structure of foreign intelligence
surveillance law, and controversial NSA programs.
[62]Learn More >
[63]MATHEMATICS

[64][jpeg]
[65]INTRODUCTION TO MATHEMATICAL THINKING
Starts September 29
In this ten week course participants will learn how professional mathematicians
solve real problems – problems that can arise from the everyday world, from
science, or from within mathematics itself. Mathematical thinking is different
from doing “school math,” which typically focuses on learning procedures to
solve highly stereotyped problems. While the key to success in “school math” is
to learn to think inside-the-box, a key aspect of mathematical thinking is to
think outside-the-box – a valuable ability in today’s world.
[66]Learn More >
[67]MEDICINE & HEALTH

[68][jpeg]
[69]MEDICAL EDUCATION IN THE NEW MILLENNIUM
Starts October 1
This eleven-week interdisciplinary course focuses on the future of medical
education, featuring talks from thought leaders and innovators from medical
education, instructional design, cognitive science, online learning, and
emerging technology. Joining the conversation will be experts from all health
care and education stakeholder domains, including patients, and students from
nursing, medicine and engineering sciences.
[70]Learn More >
[71]NATURAL & SOCIAL SCIENCES

[72][jpeg]
[73]INTRODUCTION TO THE NATURAL CAPITAL PROJECT APPROACH
Self-Paced, Starts October 15
This course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course materials
at your own pace. Discover how to integrate nature's value into decisions
affecting the environment and human well-being. Topics include an introduction
to the concepts of natural capital and ecosystem services; an overview of
InVest, a software tool for mapping, modeling, and valuing ecosystem services;
how to effectively summarize and communicate model outputs to stakeholders and
other audiences; and more.

[74]Learn More >

[75]
[76]SOCIAL AND ECONOMIC NETWORKS: MODELS AND ANALYSIS
Started September 21
Learn how to model social and economic networks and their impact on human
behavior. How do networks form, why do they exhibit certain patterns, and how
does their structure impact diffusion, learning, and other behaviors? We will
bring together models and techniques from economics, sociology, math, physics,
statistics and computer science to answer these questions.

[77]Learn More >
___________________________________________________________________________

[78]OTHER LEARNING ACTIVITIES:

[79]STATE OF THE UNION 2014
Now Open
We live in an age of rising inequality, dazzling technological innovation,
economic volatility, geopolitical uncertainty, and the accumulating impact of
climate change. These conditions confront our political leaders and us as
citizens of a democracy plagued by dysfunction. What are the implications for
the body politic? State of the Union 2014 will examine major themes that
contribute to the health, or disease, of the U.S. body politic.
[80]Learn More >

Thank you for your interest in learning opportunities available through
[81]Stanford Online. We offer many free, online courses taught by Stanford
faculty to lifelong learners worldwide for self-enrichment. We also offer a
variety of professional education opportunities in conjunction with many of the
university's schools and departments. Courses available through Stanford Online
are listed at [82]http://online.stanford.edu/courses/.

Additional courses and programs are offered by: [83]Stanford Center for
Professional Development (School of Engineering), [84]Stanford Innovation and
Entrepreneurship (Graduate School of Business and School of Engineering), and
[85]Continuing Medical Education (School of Medicine). (Registration fees may
apply.)

body_html:
NEW STANFORD ONLINE LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES!

 
BUSINESS & MANAGEMENT

 
GIVING 2.0: THE MOOC
Starts October 6
This six-week course will teach you how to assess nonprofits, create a high-impact philanthropic strategy, volunteer more effectively, use existing, free technology for good, and more.
Learn More >
ORGANIZATIONAL ANALYSIS
Self-Paced
How do we make sense of organizations and the challenges they face, let alone develop means of managing them in desired directions? This is a self-paced course that you can take at your leisure. Participants will learn multiple theories of organizational behavior and apply them to actual cases of organizational change.
Learn More >
STOCKS AND BONDS: RISKS AND RETURNS
Self-Study, Starts October 13
Stocks and bonds have always been a critical part of any investment portfolio, but what do investors actually get in exchange for their investment? Why do publicly traded stocks and bonds have value? This course presents an overview of stocks and bonds, with a focus on the finance fundamentals behind these instruments.
Learn More >
EDUCATION

 
CONSTRUCTIVE CLASSROOM CONVERSATIONS: MASTERING LANGUAGE FOR COLLEGE AND CAREER READINESS (ELEMENTARY)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course looks closely at student-to-student conversations and addresses ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions described in the new standards. It consists of four sessions with three weeks between each session. Learning in this course relies heavily on participant contributions and comments, especially in the team collaboration setting.
Learn More >
CONSTRUCTIVE CLASSROOM CONVERSATIONS: MASTERING LANGUAGE FOR COLLEGE AND CAREER READINESS (SECONDARY)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course is focused on the Common Core State Standards and Next Generation Science Standards, which emphasize improving the quality of student-to-student discourse as a major feature of instruction. The sessions and assignments are designed for participants who teach or have access to classrooms in which they can gather samples of students’ conversation during lessons, and learn ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions described in the new standards. The course includes resources and tasks for instructional coaches and others who support teachers and build school-wide capacity.
Learn More >
SUPPORTING ENGLISH LANGUAGE LEARNERS UNDER THE NEW STANDARDS
Starts October 1
In this seven-week course, participants will use a range of practical tools for gathering and analyzing language samples that show how students currently construct claims supported by evidence and/or reasoning.
Learn More >
ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

 
ALGORITHMS: DESIGN AND ANALYSIS, PART I
Starts October 13
This six-week course covers fundamental principles of algorithm design. Learn the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications to fast sorting, searching, and multiplication. Learn several blazingly fast primitives for computing on graphs, such as how to compute connectivity information and shortest paths. Study how allowing the computer to "flip coins" can lead to elegant and practical algorithms and data structures.
Learn More >
AUDIO SIGNAL PROCESSING FOR MUSIC APPLICATIONS
Starts October 1
Audio signal processing is an engineering field that focuses on the computational methods for intentionally altering sounds, methods that are used in many musical applications. This ten-week course focuses on spectral processing techniques of relevance for the description and transformation of sounds, developing the basic theoretical and practical knowledge with which to analyze, synthesize, transform and describe audio signals in the context of music applications.
Learn More >
COMPUTER SCIENCE 101
Self-Paced
This self-paced course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course materials at your own pace. Computer Science 101 teaches the essential ideas of Computer Science for a zero-prior-experience audience. Topics include the nature of computers and code, how computer hardware works, how software works, structured data, computer security, analog vs. digital, digital media, images, sounds, video, compression, and more.
Learn More >
GAME THEORY
Starts October 5
Game theory is the mathematical modeling of strategic interaction among rational (and irrational) agents. This nine-week course covers the basics: representing games and strategies, the extensive form (which computer scientists call game trees), Bayesian games (modeling things like auctions), repeated and stochastic games, and more.
Learn More >
INTRODUCTION TO CHEMICAL ENGINEERING
Self-Study
This self-study resource provides you with the opportunity to learn at your own pace. This course is an overview of Chemical Engineering conducted through discussion and engineering analysis of physical and chemical processes.Topics include overall staged separations, material and energy balances, concepts of rate process, energy and mass transport, and kinetics of chemical reactions.
Learn More >
INTRODUCTION TO HAPTICS
Self-Paced, Starts October 1
This self-paced course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course materials at your own pace. Paticipants will learn how to build, program, and control haptic devices, which are mechatronic devices that allow users to feel virtual or remote environments. Topics include an introduction to haptic technology and human haptics, how to program virtual environments, mechanical characterization and simulation, and how to assemble mechanical systems, make circuits, and program Arduino-based micro-controllers.
Learn More >
INTRODUCTION TO LOGIC
Starts September 29
Learn how to formalize information and reason systematically to produce logical conclusions in this eight-week course. Instructors will also examine logic technology and its applications — in mathematics, science, engineering, business, and law.
Learn More >
MACHINE LEARNING
Started September 22
Machine learning is the science of getting computers to act without being explicitly programmed. In this 10-week course, participants will learn about the most effective machine learning techniques, and gain practice implementing them. Topics include supervised learning, unsupervised learning, and best practices in machine learning.
Learn More >
MINING MASSIVE DATASETS
Starts September 29
This seven-week course teaches algorithms for extracting models and other information from very large amounts of data, with an emphasis on techniques that are efficient and that scale well.
Learn More >
QUANTUM MECHANICS FOR SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS
Starts September 30
This course aims to teach quantum mechanics to anyone with a reasonable college-level understanding of physical science or engineering.
Topics include an introduction to quantum mechanics, Schroedinger's wave equation, getting "quantum" behavior, quantum mechanics of systems that change in time, measurement in quantum mechanics, writing down quantum mechanics simply, the hydrogen atom, and how to solve real problems.
Learn More >
ENTREPRENEURSHIP

 
TECHNOLOGY ENTREPRENEURSHIP PART II
Started September 22
This four-week course is the second half of a course on the fundamentals of technology entrepreneurship. Topics include how to create and verify a plan for gathering resources such as talent and capital, create and verify a business model for how to sell and market an entrepreneurial idea, and generalize this process to an entrepreneurial mindset of turning problems into opportunities that can be used in larger companies and other settings.
Learn More >
LAW

 
SURVEILLANCE LAW
Starts October 14
This six-week course explores how American law facilitates electronic surveillance – but also substantially constrains it. Topics include the basics of surveillance law, applying surveillance law to information technology, compelled assistance to law enforcement, the structure of foreign intelligence surveillance law, and controversial NSA programs.
Learn More >
MATHEMATICS

 
INTRODUCTION TO MATHEMATICAL THINKING
Starts September 29
In this ten week course participants will learn how professional mathematicians solve real problems – problems that can arise from the everyday world, from science, or from within mathematics itself. Mathematical thinking is different from doing “school math,” which typically focuses on learning procedures to solve highly stereotyped problems. While the key to success in “school math” is to learn to think inside-the-box, a key aspect of mathematical thinking is to think outside-the-box – a valuable ability in today’s world.
Learn More >
MEDICINE & HEALTH

 
MEDICAL EDUCATION IN THE NEW MILLENNIUM
Starts October 1
This eleven-week interdisciplinary course focuses on the future of medical education, featuring talks from thought leaders and innovators from medical education, instructional design, cognitive science, online learning, and emerging technology. Joining the conversation will be experts from all health care and education stakeholder domains, including patients, and students from nursing, medicine and engineering sciences.
Learn More >
NATURAL & SOCIAL SCIENCES

 
INTRODUCTION TO THE NATURAL CAPITAL PROJECT APPROACH
Self-Paced, Starts October 15
This course provides you with the opportunity to interact with course materials at your own pace. Discover how to integrate nature's value into decisions affecting the environment and human well-being. Topics include an introduction to the concepts of natural capital and ecosystem services; an overview of InVest, a software tool for mapping, modeling, and valuing ecosystem services; how to effectively summarize and communicate model outputs to stakeholders and other audiences; and more.

Learn More >

 
SOCIAL AND ECONOMIC NETWORKS: MODELS AND ANALYSIS
Started September 21
Learn how to model social and economic networks and their impact on human behavior. How do networks form, why do they exhibit certain patterns, and how does their structure impact diffusion, learning, and other behaviors? We will bring together models and techniques from economics, sociology, math, physics, statistics and computer science to answer these questions.

Learn More >

OTHER LEARNING ACTIVITIES:

STATE OF THE UNION 2014
Now Open
We live in an age of rising inequality, dazzling technological innovation, economic volatility, geopolitical uncertainty, and the accumulating impact of climate change. These conditions confront our political leaders and us as citizens of a democracy plagued by dysfunction. What are the implications for the body politic? State of the Union 2014 will examine major themes that contribute to the health, or disease, of the U.S. body politic.
Learn More >
 

 
 
Thank you for your interest in learning opportunities available through Stanford Online. We offer many free, online courses taught by Stanford faculty to lifelong learners worldwide for self-enrichment. We also offer a variety of professional education opportunities in conjunction with many of the university's schools and departments. Courses available through Stanford Online are listed at http://online.stanford.edu/courses/.
 
Additional courses and programs are offered by: Stanford Center for Professional Development (School of Engineering), Stanford Innovation and Entrepreneurship (Graduate School of Business and School of Engineering), and Continuing Medical Education (School of Medicine). (Registration fees may apply.)

[1][jpeg]

[2]Stanford Online

NEW STANFORD ONLINE LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES!

[3]Learning Opportunities

[4]NATURAL AND SOCIAL SCIENCE

[5][jpeg]
[6]Social and Economic Networks: Models and Analysis
Starts September 21
Learn how to model social and economic networks and their impact on human
behavior in this nine-week course. Topics include: How do networks form, why do
they exhibit certain patterns, and how does their structure impact diffusion,
learning, and other behaviors? To address these fundamental questions, the
instruction combines models and techniques from economics, sociology, math,
physics, statistics and computer science.
[7]Learn More>
[8]EDUCATION

[9][jpeg]
[10]Constructive Classroom Conversations: Mastering Language for the Common Core
State Standards (Elementary)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course looks closely at student-to-student conversations and
addresses ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of
interactions described in the new standards. It consists of four sessions with
three weeks between each session. Learning in this course relies heavily on
participant contributions and comments, especially in the team collaboration
setting.
[11]Learn More>

[12][jpeg]
[13]Constructive Classroom Conversations: Mastering Language for the Common Core
State Standards (Secondary)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course is focused on the Common Core State Standards and Next
Generation Science Standards, which emphasize improving the quality of
student-to-student discourse as a major feature of instruction. The sessions and
assignments are designed for participants who teach or have access to classrooms
in which they can gather samples of students’ conversation during lessons, and
learn ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions
described in the new standards. The course includes resources and tasks for
instructional coaches and others who support teachers and build school-wide
capacity.
[14]Learn More>

[15][jpeg]
[16]Designing for Deeper Learning: How to Develop Performance Tasks for the
Common Core
Starts September 8
This nine-week course is designed for educators and focused on building their
capacity to use, develop, and implement curriculum-embedded performance
assessments that fit local contexts. Course activities include evaluating sample
performance tasks and developing and implementing a performance task that is
aligned with a specific curricular unit and performance outcomes. Course
instructors use a learning-centered approach where assessments are not only
about measuring learning, but are also events for learning.
[17]Learn More>

[18][jpeg]
[19]Open Knowledge: Changing the Global Course of Learning
Starts September 2
Open source, open science, open data, open access, open education, open learning
— this fourteen-week course provides an introduction to the important concept of
openness from a variety of perspectives, including education, publishing,
librarianship, economics, politics, and more, and asks you to discover what it
means to you. Open Knowledge is international and multi-institutional, and will
challenge you to take control of your own education.
[20]Learn More>
[21]ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

[22][jpeg]
[23]Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part I
Starts October 13
This six-week course covers fundamental principles of algorithm design. Learn
the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications to fast sorting,
searching, and multiplication. Learn several blazingly fast primitives for
computing on graphs, such as how to compute connectivity information and
shortest paths. And study how allowing the computer to "flip coins" can lead to
elegant and practical algorithms and data structures.
[24]Learn More>

[25][jpeg]
[26]Automata
Starts September 1
This six-week course covers four broad areas of Automata Theory: Finite automata
and regular expressions, context-free grammars, Turing machines and
decidability, and the theory of intractability, or NP-complete problems.
[27]Learn More>

[28][jpeg]
[29]Cryptography I
Starts September 8
Cryptography is an indispensable tool for protecting information in computer
systems. This six-week course explains the inner workings of cryptographic
primitives and how to correctly use them. Learn how to reason about the security
of cryptographic constructions and how to apply this knowledge to real-world
applications.
[30]Learn More>

[31][jpeg]
[32]Databases: Self-Paced
Open Now
This course is now offered as a set of smaller self-paced "mini-courses," which
will be available for an extended period of time and which can be assembled in a
variety of ways to learn about different aspects of databases. Taught by
Professor Jennifer Widom, the overall curriculum draws from Stanford's popular
Databases course. All of the mini-courses are based around video lectures and/or
video demos, and many include quizzes or a variety of automatically-checked
interactive programming exercises.
[33]Learn More>

[34][jpeg]
[35]Game Theory
Starts October 13
Game theory is the mathematical modeling of strategic interaction among rational
(and irrational) agents. This nine-week course covers the basics: representing
games and strategies, the extensive form (which computer scientists call game
trees), Bayesian games (modeling things like auctions), repeated and stochastic
games, and more.
[36]Learn More>

[37][jpeg]
[38]Introduction to Logic
Starts September 29
Learn how to formalize information and reason systematically to produce logical
conclusions in this eight-week course. Instructors will also examine logic
technology and its applications — in mathematics, science, engineering,
business, and law.
[39]Learn More>

[40][jpeg]
[41]Language, Proof and Logic
Starts September 2
The ability to reason is fundamental to human beings. Whatever the discipline or
discourse, it is important to be able to distinguish correct reasoning from
incorrect reasoning. This fifteen-week course introduces the concepts and
techniques used in logic. Instructors assume no prior exposure to the material,
and progress through discussions of the proof and model theories of
propositional and first-order logic.
[42]Learn More>

[43][jpeg]
[44]Mining Massive Datasets
Starts September 29
This seven-week course teaches algorithms for extracting models and other
information from very large amounts of data, with an emphasis on techniques that
are efficient and that scale well.
[45]Learn More>

[46][jpeg]
[47]Using GPS and Smart Phones to Create a Worldwide Laboratory and Learning
Experience
Starts October 13
This six-week course will utilize smart phones to teach the basics of satellite
navigation. A worldwide community of participants will learn GPS while
conducting "backyard" laboratory experiments on your own mobile devices. Witness
how the satellite network functions and gain a truly global understanding of
GPS.
[48]Learn More>
[49]MATHEMATICS

[50][jpeg]
[51]Introduction to Mathematical Thinking
Starts September 29
Professional mathematicians think a certain way to solve real problems –
problems that can arise from the everyday world, or from science, or from within
mathematics itself. Mathematical thinking is different from doing “school
math,” which typically focuses on learning procedures to solve highly
stereotyped problems. While the key to success in “school math” is to learn to
think inside-the-box, a key aspect of mathematical thinking is to think
outside-the-box – a valuable ability in today’s world.
[52]Learn More>
[53]MEDICINE & HEALTH

[54][jpeg]
[55]Engage and Empower Me: Patient Engagement Design
Starts September 8
Learn about participatory medicine in this nine-week course and become empowered
to create a more inclusive, collaborative healthcare system for patients. Learn
the science of habit formation, behavior change, and decision-making. Learn how
human-centered design can empower people and help them make healthy choices.
Discover how social media platforms can be used to create robust patient
communities, and how self-tracking devices can provide day-to-day data points
that motivate people to make positive changes.
[56]Learn More>

[57][jpeg]
[58]Writing in the Sciences
Starts September 2
This eight-week course teaches scientists to become more effective writers,
using practical examples and exercises. Topics include: principles of good
writing, tricks for writing faster and with less anxiety, the format of a
scientific manuscript, and issues in publication and peer review. Students from
non-science disciplines can benefit from the training provided in the first four
weeks (on general principles of effective writing).
[59]Learn More>
[60]BUSINESS AND MANAGEMENT

[61][jpeg]
[62]Stocks and Bonds: Risks and Returns: Self-Paced
Starts October 13
Stocks and bonds have always been a critical part of any investment portfolio,
but what do investors actually get in exchange for their investment? Why do
publicly traded stocks and bonds have value? This presents an overview of stocks
and bonds, with a focus on the finance fundamentals behind these instruments.
[63]Learn More>

[64][jpeg]
[65]Scaling Up Your Venture Without Screwing Up
Starts September 8
The success of every venture depends on scaling: on sustaining and enhancing its
effectiveness as it adds more employees, customers, and locations. The problem,
however, is that scaling comes with inherent risk. Even the best founders and
teams face setbacks, make mistakes, and must muddle through stretches of
confusion and uncertainty. Learn the principles that will help you scale up your
venture without screwing up in this five-week course.
[66]Learn More>

Thank you for your interest in learning opportunities available through
[67]Stanford Online. We offer many free, online courses taught by Stanford
faculty to lifelong learners worldwide for self-enrichment. We also offer a
variety of professional education opportunities in conjunction with many of the
university's schools and departments.

Additional programs and learning opportunities are offered by: [68]Stanford
Center for Professional Development (School of Engineering), [69]Stanford
Innovation and Entrepreneurship (Graduate School of Business and School of
Engineering), and [70]Continuing Medical Education (School of Medicine).
(Registration fees may apply.)

NEW STANFORD ONLINE LEARNING OPPORTUNITIES!

 

 
NATURAL AND SOCIAL SCIENCE

 
Social and Economic Networks: Models and Analysis
Starts September 21
Learn how to model social and economic networks and their impact on human behavior in this nine-week course. Topics include: How do networks form, why do they exhibit certain patterns, and how does their structure impact diffusion, learning, and other behaviors? To address these fundamental questions, the instruction combines models and techniques from economics, sociology, math, physics, statistics and computer science.
Learn More>
EDUCATION

 
Constructive Classroom Conversations: Mastering Language for the Common Core State Standards (Elementary)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course looks closely at student-to-student conversations and addresses ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions described in the new standards. It consists of four sessions with three weeks between each session. Learning in this course relies heavily on participant contributions and comments, especially in the team collaboration setting.
Learn More>
Constructive Classroom Conversations: Mastering Language for the Common Core State Standards (Secondary)
Starts October 1
This fourteen-week course is focused on the Common Core State Standards and Next Generation Science Standards, which emphasize improving the quality of student-to-student discourse as a major feature of instruction. The sessions and assignments are designed for participants who teach or have access to classrooms in which they can gather samples of students’ conversation during lessons, and learn ways to improve students' abilities to engage in the types of interactions described in the new standards. The course includes resources and tasks for instructional coaches and others who support teachers and build school-wide capacity.
Learn More>
Designing for Deeper Learning: How to Develop Performance Tasks for the Common Core
Starts September 8
This nine-week course is designed for educators and focused on building their capacity to use, develop, and implement curriculum-embedded performance assessments that fit local contexts. Course activities include evaluating sample performance tasks and developing and implementing a performance task that is aligned with a specific curricular unit and performance outcomes. Course instructors use a learning-centered approach where assessments are not only about measuring learning, but are also events for learning.
Learn More>
Open Knowledge: Changing the Global Course of Learning
Starts September 2
Open source, open science, open data, open access, open education, open learning — this fourteen-week course provides an introduction to the important concept of openness from a variety of perspectives, including education, publishing, librarianship, economics, politics, and more, and asks you to discover what it means to you. Open Knowledge is international and multi-institutional, and will challenge you to take control of your own education.
Learn More>
ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

 
Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part I
Starts October 13
This six-week course covers fundamental principles of algorithm design. Learn the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications to fast sorting, searching, and multiplication. Learn several blazingly fast primitives for computing on graphs, such as how to compute connectivity information and shortest paths. And study how allowing the computer to "flip coins" can lead to elegant and practical algorithms and data structures.
Learn More>
Automata
Starts September 1
This six-week course covers four broad areas of Automata Theory: Finite automata and regular expressions, context-free grammars, Turing machines and decidability, and the theory of intractability, or NP-complete problems.
Learn More>
Cryptography I
Starts September 8
Cryptography is an indispensable tool for protecting information in computer systems. This six-week course explains the inner workings of cryptographic primitives and how to correctly use them. Learn how to reason about the security of cryptographic constructions and how to apply this knowledge to real-world applications.
Learn More>
Databases: Self-Paced
Open Now
This course is now offered as a set of smaller self-paced "mini-courses," which will be available for an extended period of time and which can be assembled in a variety of ways to learn about different aspects of databases. Taught by Professor Jennifer Widom, the overall curriculum draws from Stanford's popular Databases course. All of the mini-courses are based around video lectures and/or video demos, and many include quizzes or a variety of automatically-checked interactive programming exercises.
Learn More>
Game Theory
Starts October 13
Game theory is the mathematical modeling of strategic interaction among rational (and irrational) agents. This nine-week course covers the basics: representing games and strategies, the extensive form (which computer scientists call game trees), Bayesian games (modeling things like auctions), repeated and stochastic games, and more.
Learn More>
Introduction to Logic
Starts September 29
Learn how to formalize information and reason systematically to produce logical conclusions in this eight-week course. Instructors will also examine logic technology and its applications — in mathematics, science, engineering, business, and law.
Learn More>
Language, Proof and Logic
Starts September 2
The ability to reason is fundamental to human beings. Whatever the discipline or discourse, it is important to be able to distinguish correct reasoning from incorrect reasoning. This fifteen-week course introduces the concepts and techniques used in logic. Instructors assume no prior exposure to the material, and progress through discussions of the proof and model theories of propositional and first-order logic.
Learn More>
Mining Massive Datasets
Starts September 29
This seven-week course teaches algorithms for extracting models and other information from very large amounts of data, with an emphasis on techniques that are efficient and that scale well.
Learn More>
Using GPS and Smart Phones to Create a Worldwide Laboratory and Learning Experience
Starts October 13
This six-week course will utilize smart phones to teach the basics of satellite navigation. A worldwide community of participants will learn GPS while conducting "backyard" laboratory experiments on your own mobile devices. Witness how the satellite network functions and gain a truly global understanding of GPS.
Learn More>
MATHEMATICS

 
Introduction to Mathematical Thinking
Starts September 29
Professional mathematicians think a certain way to solve real problems – problems that can arise from the everyday world, or from science, or from within mathematics itself. Mathematical thinking is different from doing “school math,” which typically focuses on learning procedures to solve highly stereotyped problems. While the key to success in “school math” is to learn to think inside-the-box, a key aspect of mathematical thinking is to think outside-the-box – a valuable ability in today’s world.
Learn More>
MEDICINE & HEALTH

 
Engage and Empower Me: Patient Engagement Design
Starts September 8
Learn about participatory medicine in this nine-week course and become empowered to create a more inclusive, collaborative healthcare system for patients. Learn the science of habit formation, behavior change, and decision-making. Learn how human-centered design can empower people and help them make healthy choices. Discover how social media platforms can be used to create robust patient communities, and how self-tracking devices can provide day-to-day data points that motivate people to make positive changes.
Learn More>
Writing in the Sciences
Starts September 2
This eight-week course teaches scientists to become more effective writers, using practical examples and exercises. Topics include: principles of good writing, tricks for writing faster and with less anxiety, the format of a scientific manuscript, and issues in publication and peer review. Students from non-science disciplines can benefit from the training provided in the first four weeks (on general principles of effective writing).
Learn More>
BUSINESS AND MANAGEMENT

 
Stocks and Bonds: Risks and Returns: Self-Paced
Starts October 13
Stocks and bonds have always been a critical part of any investment portfolio, but what do investors actually get in exchange for their investment? Why do publicly traded stocks and bonds have value? This presents an overview of stocks and bonds, with a focus on the finance fundamentals behind these instruments.
Learn More>
Scaling Up Your Venture Without Screwing Up
Starts September 8
The success of every venture depends on scaling: on sustaining and enhancing its effectiveness as it adds more employees, customers, and locations. The problem, however, is that scaling comes with inherent risk. Even the best founders and teams face setbacks, make mistakes, and must muddle through stretches of confusion and uncertainty. Learn the principles that will help you scale up your venture without screwing up in this five-week course.
Learn More>
 

 
 
Thank you for your interest in learning opportunities available through Stanford Online. We offer many free, online courses taught by Stanford faculty to lifelong learners worldwide for self-enrichment. We also offer a variety of professional education opportunities in conjunction with many of the university's schools and departments.
 
Additional programs and learning opportunities are offered by: Stanford Center for Professional Development (School of Engineering), Stanford Innovation and Entrepreneurship (Graduate School of Business and School of Engineering), and Continuing Medical Education (School of Medicine). (Registration fees may apply.)

New Stanford Online Learning Opportunities!

Business & Management

[1]Organizational Analysis

Design & Creativity

[2]Creativity: Music to my Ears

Education

Engineering & Computer Science

[8]Algorithms II

[9]Machine Learning

Environment & Energy

[10]Reservoir Geomechanics

UPCOMING COURSES

"With the power to cross borders and languages, music serves as a compelling
tool for unlocking creative potential."

[11]Creativity: Music to my Ears
with Tina Seelig

BUSINESS & MANAGEMENT

[12]Organizational Analysis

March 31: Daniel McFarland

It is hard to imagine living in modern society without participating in or
interacting with organizations. How do we make sense of organizations and the
challenges they face, let alone develop means of managing them in desired
directions? Every week, you’ll learn a different organizational theory, and it
will become a lens through which you can interpret concrete organizational
situations. [13]....more

DESIGN & CREATIVITY

[14]Creativity: Music to my Ears

Starts April 2: Tina Seelig

Creativity: Music to My Ears is a six-week course designed to explore several
factors that stimulate creativity in individuals, teams, and organizations. All
of the projects in this experiential course will deal with some aspect of music,
including listening, creating, and sharing. No musical talent is required – just
an interest in exploring the role that music plays in our lives. [15]....more

EDUCATION

[16]Mastering Language for the Common Core State Standards:

[17]​Focus on Mathematics in the Elementary Grades

March 26: Kenji Hakuta
In this course teachers will be provided with a range of practical tools for
gathering and analyzing language samples that show how students learn and what
supports they need in elementary math classrooms. [18]....more

[19]How to Learn Math: For Teachers and Parents

($125 fee)

Starts June 15: Jo Boaler

Explore the new research ideas on mathematics learning and student mindsets that
can transform students' experiences with math. The sessions are all interactive
and include various thinking tasks to promote active engagement - such as
reflecting on videos, designing lessons, and discussing ideas with peers.
[20]....more

ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

[21]Cryptography I

April 1: Dan Boneh

Cryptography is an indispensable tool for protecting information in computer
systems. This course explains the inner workings of cryptographic primitives and
how to correctly use them. Students will learn how to reason about the security
of cryptographic constructions and how to apply this knowledge to real-world
applications. [22]...more

[23]General Game Playing

March 31: Michael Genesereth

General game players are computer systems able to play strategy games based
solely on formal game descriptions supplied at "runtime". This course is an
introduction to General Game Playing (GGP).  Students will get an introduction
to the theory of General Game Playing and will learn how to create GGP programs
capable of competing against other programs and humans. [24]....more

[25]Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part I

Starts April 29: Tim Roughgarden

In this course you will learn several fundamental principles of algorithm
design. You'll learn the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications
to fast sorting, searching, and multiplication. Learn the answers to questions
such as: How do data structures like heaps, hash tables, bloom filters, and
balanced search trees actually work, anyway?  [26]....more

[27]Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part II

Starts June 30: Tim Roughgarden

In this course you will learn several fundamental principles of advanced
algorithm design: greedy algorithms and applications; dynamic programming and
applications; NP-completeness and what it means for the algorithm designer; the
design and analysis of heuristics; and more. [28]....more

Machine Learning

Coming in June: Andrew Ng

Machine learning is the science of getting computers to act without being
explicitly programmed. In the past decade, machine learning has given us
self-driving cars, practical speech recognition, effective web search, and a
vastly improved understanding of the human genome. In this class, you will learn
about the most effective machine learning techniques, and gain practice
implementing them and getting them to work for yourself. ....more

ENVIRONMENT & ENERGY

[29]Reservoir Geomechanics

April 1: Mark Zoback

This interdisciplinary course encompasses the fields of rock mechanics,
structural geology, earthquake seismology and petroleum engineering to address a
wide range of geomechanical problems that arise during the exploitation of oil
and gas reservoirs. The course is intended for geoscientists and engineers in
the petroleum and geothermal industries, and for research scientists interested
in stress measurements and their application to problems of faulting and fluid
flow in the crust. [30]....more

Participation

UPCOMING COURSES

 

 
"With the power to cross borders and languages, music serves as a compelling tool for unlocking creative potential." 
 
Creativity: Music to my Ears
with Tina Seelig
 
 

BUSINESS & MANAGEMENT

Organizational Analysis

March 31: Daniel McFarland

It is hard to imagine living in modern society without participating in or interacting with organizations. How do we make sense of organizations and the challenges they face, let alone develop means of managing them in desired directions? Every week, you’ll learn a different organizational theory, and it will become a lens through which you can interpret concrete organizational situations. ....more

DESIGN & CREATIVITY

Creativity: Music to my Ears

Starts April 2: Tina Seelig

Creativity: Music to My Ears is a six-week course designed to explore several factors that stimulate creativity in individuals, teams, and organizations. All of the projects in this experiential course will deal with some aspect of music, including listening, creating, and sharing. No musical talent is required – just an interest in exploring the role that music plays in our lives. ....more 

 

EDUCATION

Mastering Language for the Common Core State Standards:

​Focus on Mathematics in the Elementary Grades 

March 26: Kenji Hakuta

In this course teachers will be provided with a range of practical tools for gathering and analyzing language samples that show how students learn and what supports they need in elementary math classrooms. ....more 
 

How to Learn Math: For Teachers and Parents

($125 fee)

Starts June 15: Jo Boaler

Explore the new research ideas on mathematics learning and student mindsets that can transform students' experiences with math. The sessions are all interactive and include various thinking tasks to promote active engagement - such as reflecting on videos, designing lessons, and discussing ideas with peers. ....more 

 

ENGINEERING & COMPUTER SCIENCE

Cryptography I

April 1: Dan Boneh

Cryptography is an indispensable tool for protecting information in computer systems. This course explains the inner workings of cryptographic primitives and how to correctly use them. Students will learn how to reason about the security of cryptographic constructions and how to apply this knowledge to real-world applications. ....more

General Game Playing

March 31: Michael Genesereth

General game players are computer systems able to play strategy games based solely on formal game descriptions supplied at "runtime". This course is an introduction to General Game Playing (GGP).  Students will get an introduction to the theory of General Game Playing and will learn how to create GGP programs capable of competing against other programs and humans. ....more

Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part I

Starts April 29: Tim Roughgarden

In this course you will learn several fundamental principles of algorithm design. You'll learn the divide-and-conquer design paradigm, with applications to fast sorting, searching, and multiplication. Learn the answers to questions such as: How do data structures like heaps, hash tables, bloom filters, and balanced search trees actually work, anyway?  ....more

Algorithms: Design and Analysis, Part II

Starts June 30: Tim Roughgarden

In this course you will learn several fundamental principles of advanced algorithm design: greedy algorithms and applications; dynamic programming and applications; NP-completeness and what it means for the algorithm designer; the design and analysis of heuristics; and more. ....more

Machine Learning

Coming in June: Andrew Ng

Machine learning is the science of getting computers to act without being explicitly programmed. In the past decade, machine learning has given us self-driving cars, practical speech recognition, effective web search, and a vastly improved understanding of the human genome. In this class, you will learn about the most effective machine learning techniques, and gain practice implementing them and getting them to work for yourself. ....more

ENVIRONMENT & ENERGY

Reservoir Geomechanics

April 1: Mark Zoback

This interdisciplinary course encompasses the fields of rock mechanics, structural geology, earthquake seismology and petroleum engineering to address a wide range of geomechanical problems that arise during the exploitation of oil and gas reservoirs. The course is intended for geoscientists and engineers in the petroleum and geothermal industries, and for research scientists interested in stress measurements and their application to problems of faulting and fluid flow in the crust. ....more

Thank you for your interest in learning opportunities available through Stanford Online. We offer many free, online courses taught by Stanford faculty to lifelong learners worldwide. We also offer a variety of professional education opportunities in conjunction with many of the University’s schools and departments. Courses available through Stanford Online are listed at http://online.stanford.edu/courses/.


Additional courses are offered by: Stanford Center for Professional Development (School of Engineering), Stanford Innovation and Entrepreneurship (Graduate School of Business and School of Engineering), and Continuing Medical Education (School of Medicine). (Registration fees may apply.)
